translation.content: Long before SoundCloud made him famous, RJ Glasgow was at home producing edits. From his bedroom in Philadelphia, the artist now known as LSDXOXO spliced R&B classics with DMX Krew and Madonna to show off to his peers in the playground. Primed for the rise of edit culture, he soon graduated from bedroom producer to internet provocateur with the launch of his online mix series, Spit or Swallow, in 2010, mashing up La Roux with Ratatat and Rihanna with Crystal Castles. Nearly a decade on, that same DIY sensibility has propelled Glasgow to places far beyond his hometown and the online realm. The Berlin-based artist has opened for Beyoncé on her Renaissance tour, remixed Kelela, Lady Gaga and Björk, and taken his Floorgasm party across several continents. Today, he represents a uniquely modern vision of club music, where pop, techno and queerness coexist on equal footing. In doing so, he's mastered something rare: becoming a star while still staying true to the defiantly unorthodox elements that made him such an enfant terrible in the first place. Glasgow's RA Mix captures him at this moment. In just under an hour, RA.1012 tears through many shades of contemporary techno, from no-nonsense (Adam Beyer) to loopy hardgroove (Italia 90, River Moon) to, of course, a few sharp edits (EsDeeKid & Fakemink). The whole thing moves at a relentless pace—26 tracks in 57 minutes, to be exact. "Maybe my music invokes that freaky feeling," he told Resident Advisor back in 2022, when he became our first-ever cover star. "A lot of people have that inner freak." Returning to the mixtape format that first gave him fame, that blend of sensuality, deviant humour and defiant self-possession is alive and well on RA.1012. It shows that for all his globetrotting, Glasgow will always be a club kid. - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Lately I've been pouring my energy into the compilation record I'm preparing, along with the other artists I've aligned with, for the proper launch of my imprint label. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? I recorded the mix in my flat here in Berlin. The idea behind the mix was to showcase some of my taste in traditional techno, along with some of the more contemporary and explorational selections I've made lately. You've included special edits and remixes of your own tracks. What drew you to revisiting your own material in this way? The remixes I've included here of my own tracks were meant to shine a light on the curatorial aspects that come along with being a selector. Some of the remixes here have been released via the remix competition I put on around the release of my most recent EP, DGTL ANML. The others will be released on the compilation record I mentioned. When we last spoke for your RA Cover in 2022, you were preparing to take a creative risk with your debut album. How has your relationship to risk and reinvention changed since then? Overall, I believe the creative risks that I've been taking in my career have helped a great deal to establish me as a more rounded musician—as well as a more developed one. The pressures that can come along with a debut project have definitely been alleviated with its release. My relationship with reinvention will always be an important one. It's needed to maintain excitement within the process. You talked then about wanting to move beyond the "bangers-only" tag. Do you feel like this mix shows where that evolution has led you? Definitely. Releasing and mixing bangers isn't something I want to stop doing. I've just always wanted to have my sets feel like more of a lesson in my multiple influences, and I do believe this mix encapsulates that desire. Collaboration seems central to your work—what makes a creative partnership click for you? Collaboration has been pretty much essential for the entirety of my musical career. I'm very intentional about the artists I choose to collaborate with because I think they all lend themselves to the storytelling aspect of my work. Choosing artists to work with that have a similar approach to expressing themselves is generally what holds most of the importance in the process. You've been part of scenes in Philadelphia, New York, Berlin and beyond. How have those experiences shaped your sound? Living in all of these cities has certainly helped to shape my sound in a way. Growing up in Philly, and then moving to New York in early adulthood is probably why my music feels so explorative. The scenes in both cities made me realise early on in my career that genre bounds aren't always necessary. My move to Berlin helped me to become more technically skilled in music making. I like to take the experiences I build in the places I've lived, and turn them into a contribution of my own. What are you looking forward to in the near future? I'm looking forward to the establishment of my imprint, as well as creating my next album. There's more development that I want to take on as an artist, and I'm anticipating that. ⁠What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? GHE20G0TH1K for sure. Venus X, its founder, was pretty much my mentor in the earlier stages of my DJ career. Both she and the event series made me understand how production value really can mold the experience for attendees. She's also a creative who believes in the teachable aspects of musical curation. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? I'd like for the world to better understand the healing that club experiences can have. I think nightlife is perceived as inherently debaucherous in the public eye, but it's proven time and time again that its true essence is in community.
translation.blurb: 57 minutes of sharp, fast, freaky techno from the underground pop star.
